data.title: Ethereum At Risk Of Falling Under $996
data.description:
My forecast that Bitcoin (BTC-USD) would plunge to $60,000 after the January FOMC meeting played out as expected. That is, BTC gained significant downward momentum on January 30, 2026, two days after the meeting, and reached a low of $59,930 on February 6, 2026.

With that move and my first target of $60,000 now realized, my broader bearish thesis remains intact, and I continue to target BTC at $30,000 by the end of this crypto winter. For Ethereum (ETH-USD), the corresponding bottom should be below $996, as I’ll demonstrate in this article.

Core Macro Trends Still Firm

I remain bearish because the sector rotation out of crypto (risk-off sentiment), resulting from the normalization of both real yields and liquidity (discussed in my articles here and here), is still in play and will remain so unless there are major macroeconomic changes – a highly unlikely scenario for the foreseeable future given the Fed’s latest economic projections that indicate robust growth, benign inflation, and normal unemployment.

Below are two charts; one compares ETH prices against real yield changes (referred to as RYC going forward) and money supply (M2) changes (referred to as M2C).

These two charts clearly depict ETH’s inverse relation to RYC and direct relationship to M2C, albeit with a lag, supporting my view that normalization of these two broader macroeconomic metrics is the primary cause of the crypto winter that we are in at present.

I won’t, however, go into the details of BTC’s and, by extension, ETH’s relation to RYC and M2C because I’ve already done so in my articles linked above. If you haven’t read those, I recommend that you do so before continuing.

A Discrepancy

Next, consider the chart below, which compares ETH’s prices against BTC’s since November 2017.

Although BTC and ETH demonstrate a strong statistical correlation, a major discrepancy is observable. Bitcoin’s second all-time high of $124,752 was nearly twice its previous peak of $67,566, whereas Ethereum’s second high at $4,831 exceeded its earlier top of $4,812 by only ~0.39%.

Uncovering the reasons for this discrepancy is the crux of my analysis and will eventually allow me to set a price of price target of less than $996.

Peaks and Troughs

Before the two crypto bull runs since 2019, ETH and BTC had bottomed out on January 29, 2019, at $105 and $3,448, respectively.

Following that, on November 8, 2021, BTC attained its then-all-time high of $67,566, while ETH reached its peak at $4,812. The fact that ETH appreciated forty-five times from its bottom, whereas BTC appreciated “only” nineteen times, indicates that forces other than a risk-on sentiment were in play for ETH.

To be specific, ETH’s peak in 2021 was in large part due to the DeFi summer, characterized by an explosion of decentralized finance apps and, consequently, a surge in on-chain transaction volume that caused not only gas prices to explode (chart below) but also an indirect demand for ETH for its use as collateral in lending and liquidity pools.

Furthermore, testing for the Beacon chain commenced on December 1, 2020, which was a strong indicator that moving to Proof of Stake was no longer just talk but a reality.

You don’t need to understand the technicalities behind that change, only that the Beacon test decisively signaled that a major reduction in the ETH supply is coming. And as with most things in life, when supply decreases and demand remains elevated, as it did during the DeFi summer, prices rise.

Then came the crypto crash, led by changes in RYC and M2C expectations, causing ETH to fall to $993 on June 18, 2022, and BTC’s drop to $16,439 on December 19, 2022. ETH crashed earlier than BTC due to the collapse of Celsius Network and Three Arrows Capital. I won’t go into the details of those failures because they have no bearing on my thesis.

As for tackling the congestion crisis caused by DeFi summer, work on it began with the introduction of fee burning through the EIP-1559 Implementation on August 5, 2021, but the problem was not completely resolved until the Dencun upgrade in March 2024. That is, with the Dencun upgrade, transaction fees on Layer 2 dropped by 90-95%, eventually causing ETH prices to crash.

The primary takeaway here is that it was not until March 2024 that congestion and fee effects were priced out, meaning, on a fundamental level, any high or low before that was larger than what it should have been if ETH were to exist in the state that it does now. That explains why, during the second crypto rally, ETH’s prices were only marginally higher than its previous high.

With that occurrence and the logic behind it taken into consideration, it’s also reasonable to assume that ETH's trough will also only be marginally higher than at the end of that first crypto rally. As mentioned before, the trough was at $993, which means that ETH's new fundamental floor is ~$996.92 (1.0039 multiplied by $993).

Why not $1,500 witnessed in March 2024 as the base? Because at that time, risk-on sentiment due to elevated RYC and MC2 was prevalent – not the case at present or at the time of the $993 trough, making the latter a better benchmark.

Trending Towards Net Inflation

Note that in the previous section I did not claim that price magnification due to the Beacon upgrade, and thus a net deflationary supply, has also been priced out. That's because ETH until now has remained net deflationary, but I suspect that to change in the coming months.

I claim so based on ETH’s supply trend for the past few years (chart below) and the early signal it's providing, i.e., a move from a state of deflation (issuance less than burn – supply contraction) to inflation (issuance greater than burn – excess supply).

That said, average and total ETH burned per year have also demonstrated a downtrend (charts below). Thus, it remains to be seen whether ETH will reach a state of net inflation, and more importantly, whether that will lead to meaningful changes in prices.

Also, if you believe that ETH cannot enter a net inflation stage, you would be wrong in your assumption, because the event has already happened once, in April 2025; see the chart below.

Also note that the average burn rate in that week, at 91 per day, was much higher than the average burn rate of 56 per day that has been observed YTD 2026.

An argument against all that has been presented in this section could be that ETH prices rallied even when it was inflationary, i.e., before the introduction of fee burn and Proof of Stake.

That’s a valid argument, but I’m not saying that ETH cannot rise in an inflationary situation, only that its price will and should adjust if it turns net inflationary as opposed to net deflationary.

Stated differently, since September 16, 2022, when ETH became net deflationary for the first time, it has been treated as a scarce asset, arguably more so than BTC because the latter’s supply is fixed, not decreasing. But if and when there are indications that ETH’s supply may increase for the foreseeable future, its price should be altered to incorporate that fact.

Thesis Risk: New Projects

An argument against, and the primary risk to my thesis, is that the burn rate may rise due to new and promising ETH projects in 2026 and beyond. While that would be a fair argument, the likelihood of such an event remains low because KPMG’s Venture Pulse Report for Q4 2025 states that most venture capital money is flowing into AI startups.

Nonetheless, total money invested in pre-seed startups, a category that all new blockchain startups will fall under, is larger than it was in 2020, at the height of the DeFi summer. However, a reasonable assumption is that AI startups are the primary beneficiaries of all those early-stage funds.

Also, a risk-off sentiment, which in turn is dictated by RYC and M2C, also affects VC funding activities because it is among the riskiest of all investments. This is to say that numbers presented in that report should not be taken at face value because 2026 brings a new liquidity regime where VC funding may move towards late-stage startups that are deemed less risky.

Last but not least, L2 (Layer-2) of ETH was implemented such that another DeFi summer induced congestion event does not ever occur, indicating that it would take a revolutionary ETH-based project to increase burn rate.

Closing Thoughts

The big takeaway of this entire thesis is that investors should not jump on the ETH bandwagon when this crypto season is over, because its lowest value, while most likely to be less than $996, is hard to determine with all the unknown variables at present.

I also want to state that the next crypto crash is unlikely to occur following the next FOMC meeting, as markets have already priced in the expectation that there will be few, if any, rate cuts in 2026 – particularly after a 130K increase in January 2026 payrolls and a January CPI print of 2.4%, suggesting that downside risks to both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate are diminishing at the current Fed Funds rate, eliminating the need to tweak it.

That said, I expect both BTC and ETH to drop to new lows of ~$55,000 and ~$1,500, before bouncing back to their respective floors of $60,000 and $1,500, after the next Fed meeting on March 18, 2026.

Mainly because Chairman Powell, in the last FOMC meeting, clearly stated that the Fed will assess incoming data to determine whether another rate cut is required. Colloquially speaking, another decision to hold rates steady will be the final indicator for a rate cut pause, but as of now the market cannot know for sure and will assume otherwise until it's official.

When that temporary collapse and bounce back does happen, most media outlets will term it as the end of the crypto winter, and the narrative will most likely move from the sentiment at present that “crypto is in freefall, don’t buy” to “markets have bottomed, time to buy again.” But that would be a false claim, and I advise readers not to enter a position at those prices.

data.title: Danaos: Alaska LNG Investment - Smart Diversification Or Opportunity Cost?
data.description:
Danaos Corporation (DAC) is now trading over the $100 share price ceiling. If you’ve followed me covering the stock in recent years, you know this moment was long overdue. I’ve covered the company 13 times since my first piece on it on Seeking Alpha in Sep 2021, which was titled "Danaos: About To Sail To $100 And Beyond". It did in 2022, but then spent years trying to get back up.

Of course, there were times I’ve been frustrated with DAC's performance, like in this April 2024 article, when I made the case for finding it appropriate to take the exit. However, I’ve generally remained bullish and consistently supported that DAC is deeply undervalued. So finally seeing it move back above $100 again makes me feel like the world is in the right place.

What's making the update I'm writing now quite special is that Danaos has decided to sail far from its home waters (containership and drybulks) to the LNG space. That's quite a major change for a company I have always thought of as a pure containership play (with a bit of dry bulk exposure after it entered the space a few years ago).

We already know the containership fleet is set to keep generating predictable revenue for years to come. Apart from CapEx to recycle the fleet over time, I would have expected the company to accelerate capital returns. Hopefully, that would also re-rate the stock higher. Now this investment changes the whole conversation.

Core Operations

Before I discuss the LNG investment, let me briefly touch on the core business. There is nothing surprising here. Quarterly revenues reached a new record of around $266 million, driven by the containership fleet being effectively fully booked at excellent rates. You can easily expect more of the same here over the medium term, as 100% of 2026 operating days and 87% of 2027 days are already locked in at highly profitable rates. The contracted revenue backlog has now reached a huge $4.3 billion, providing envy-worthy earnings visibility.

I also owe some credit to the dry bulk segment, which I had previously viewed as a distraction. I would still prefer if Danaos had stuck with containership, but at least we know the dry bulk vessels are contributing to profits. The division posted adjusted EBITDA of $12.9 million, nearly doubling YoY following near-perfect 99.8% utilization.

The balance remained pristine in the meantime, with Danaos' cash position surpassing $1 billion and net leverage at a tiny 0.20x Net Debt/EBITDA. The key question, therefore, continues to revolve around where all this cash will go.

Danaos can continue to expand/renew its fleet using cash from operations, and, as many other analysts and I have argued here on Seeking Alpha for years, the stock remains deeply undervalued. Will we finally see accelerated buybacks or a tender offer at these levels? Well, management had a different idea. Let's examine it.

The Alaska LNG Move

The answer to where Danaos can best deploy its cash pile arrived a few days before its Q4 results. In late January, the company announced a $50 million strategic investment in the Alaska LNG project through a partnership with Glenfarne Group, effectively marking its entry into the LNG space. Under the deal, Danaos becomes the "preferred tonnage provider" for at least six LNG carriers required for the project.

What is the rationale for this move? I would assume that management feels that investing further in the containership sector, which will face a massive wave of newbuild deliveries in 2027, might lead to over-concentration. After all, Danaos already has many more newbuilds hitting the water through 2026-2028, and all of them have been locked in at exceptional rates, some running as far away as 2038.

The LNG investment will allow Danaos to diversify into another area, where charters typically feature even longer multi-decade terms that resemble long-term infrastructure bonds. You can take a look at Capital Clean Energy Carriers (CCEC) as an example. When I covered it back in October, I mentioned how its time-charter book extended to 118 years (not a typo) if all options were to be exercised, precisely because of how long these contracts are.

But does this move add risk to the business? I think so. In the news release, Danaos said it will "operate" these vessels. In shipping, "operate" comes with assuming technical management of the vessel. Danaos will thus be responsible for the specialized crews and complex maintenance required to transport LNG. Danaos knows what it’s doing when it comes to managing container ships; I don't question that. But LNG might prove to be different. I don't really know too much about these procedures, but I can imagine that dealing with crews, maintenance, insurance, and the safety standards that come with handling gas at -162°C sounds like a different game that requires different expertise.

At the very least, there is a point to make that the move into LNG can once again be a distraction from the core business. We know the management is top-tier in the container shipping space, so it's easy to argue that staying in their lane would be the better approach. But Danaos' management is likely looking for longevity here and ensuring they are building a business for the next few decades. After all, the bond-like multi-year cash flow visibility that can come from these LNG deals does follow the same logic they've been following with the containerships, particularly the newbuilds, which, as I mentioned earlier, have already been attached to decade-long contracts.

The Capital Allocation Debate

The main criticism I have about the LNG move has to do with the more general capital allocation rationale, particularly as Danaos trades at a still-depressed, in my opinion, valuation. I get that reasons might include diversifying away from an already very heavy exposure to containerships and/or focusing on building a company with a multi-decade operating horizon ahead, but when the opportunity cost of buying back your own stock is so wide, it warrants scrutiny.

Even if Danaos can achieve an optimistic low double-digit return on the LNG investment (which carries operating risks), it might pale in comparison to the return from buying back DAC stock. At a 0.5x P/B ratio, every dollar spent on a buyback yields an immediate, nearly risk-free return that is already much higher.

To be fair, management did repurchase $75.7 million worth of stock during the full year, up from $53.3 million in FY2024. And that is a decent amount for a $2 billion company. But why not more? They clearly don't want to "shrink" the company by contracting the balance sheet. Still, capital allocation remains a forever debate when it comes to Danaos.

What I Am Doing

I continue to like Danaos a lot, even though I am not currently holding the stock. I sold a put a few months ago at $85 in hopes of getting in at a superb price (yes, I am this greedy). The premium was around $5.50, so it was a great deal. Following the recent rally, I bought the put back at about 10% of its original value. However, I still want some exposure in the company, given that the price remains disconnected from book value. If there is a pullback in the near term, especially below $100/share, I will likely re-enter.

The LNG move, regardless of how it plays out, should remain a small part of the core business. Whether Danaos could have realized higher returns for shareholders by buying back stock more aggressively remains to be seen (and is likely), but at the very worst, this should be a boring, bond-like stake to go along with the massive container-ship charter backlog that has been fueling the bullish case for years and is set to do so for many years ahead.

data.title: Crispr: Emphasis Shifts To Cardiovascular Pipeline Amid Slow CASGEVY Launch
data.description:
Overview

I’ve covered CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) for quite a while now. Until just recently, my focus has predominantly been on its ex vivo sickle cell disease gene therapy product, CASGEVY, which it co-markets with Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX). CASGEVY’s launch has underwhelmed – and the two are still in the red (expenses > revenues).

In my most recent write-up, the focus shifted more towards CRISPR’s pipeline, which includes in vivo gene editing, RNA therapies, and CAR-T. I was interested in CTX310, in particular, for dyslipidemia and cardiovascular disease. This is an area that has garnered attention following Eli Lilly’s (LLY) $1.3 billion acquisition of Verve Therapeutics. In a Phase 1 trial that was published in NEJM, CTX310 reduced LDL-C and triglycerides on top of maximally tolerated background therapy after just one edit.

While I remained cautious on CASGEVY’s prospects and CRISPR’s high cash burn, I figured that its pipeline breadth and ~$1.94 billion in cash and investments balanced it out, meriting a Hold rating.

The article that follows reviews CRISPR’s Q4 and FY25 earnings, revealed last week.

CASGEVY

While CASGEVY generated $54 million at Vertex in Q4, CRISPR didn’t recognize any of that in its income statement. That is because the product’s costs continue to outpace its revenue. Bear in mind that under their 60/40 (Vertex/CRISPR) split, CRISPR only begins to recognize revenue once Vertex has recouped certain shared launch and manufacturing costs. The company did report that “64 patients received infusions of CASGEVY during the year, including 30 in the fourth quarter.” So it does appear to be accelerating. The focus continues to be on increasing access geographically and strengthening CASGEVY’s dataset in hemoglobinopathies.

The Numbers

CRISPR’s “loss from operations” has grown in a step-like manner, increasing from $222.5 million to $466.6 million to $664.6 million between 2023-2025. For FY25, this was mostly a result of higher “collaboration expenses” (related to CASGEVY’s shared launch costs) and acquired in-process research and development.

Still, CRISPR maintains a healthy cash and investment balance - $1.976 billion at year-end - which should afford it (at least) 3-4 years of runway (this is an estimate).

Pipeline

With so much money pouring into R&D, the onus is on pipeline developments.

Following the proof-of-concept data for CTX310, CRISPR is prioritizing severe hypertriglyceridemia and refractory hypercholesterolemia. So, relative to the general high-cholesterol patient population, these are two specific high-unmet-need populations.

I haven’t talked much about CTX611 in the past, so I figured I would give it a review.

611 is described as a “small interfering RNA,” or siRNA, therapy that was developed through a partnership with Sirius Therapeutics. 611 targets the Factor XI protein, which contributes to the development of clots but is also largely unnecessary for the body’s ability to stop bleeding. Although there are a few traditional blood thinners, they require daily dosing (which can result in compliance issues) and can have side effects like bleeding.

So 611 is effectively trying to solve two problems. One, its infrequent subcutaneous dosing - potentially only twice a year - would help in the compliance department. And its mechanism of action - acting like a “dimmer switch” at the genetic level - could lower bleeding risk.

CRISPR is “piloting” 611 in a Phase 2 total knee arthroplasty trial (NCT07140523). A common post-operative complication with TKAs is blood clots. So 611 is looking to prevent this. 611 is being compared to enoxaparin, which is dosed daily and administered subcutaneously.

Pending TKA results (expected later this year), CRISPR intends to evaluate 611 in other contexts as well.

Of course, as this is a multi-billion dollar market, a few others are testing “next-generation” blood thinners, especially Factor XI inhibitors.

Given the compliance issue I cited earlier - which I see in practice as an RN too often - 611 really stands out in the crowd for its potential for biannual dosing. I’ve seen many patients come back to the hospital with complications because they were, for one reason or another, unable to comply with their blood thinner regimen.

In this light, Phase 2 TKA data could be a major catalyst for CRISPR. The economics for 611 are favorable. After up to $300 million in milestone payments and mid-single digits to low-double digits on all future global sales, CRISPR practically owns the rights to 611.

While it is still quite early, I gave my best attempt at modeling 611 - just in TKA, though, to give an idea of the opportunity ahead.

Estimates vary, but there are “nearly 1.3 million” knee replacements performed in the U.S. each year. Across other VTE prophylaxis-related conditions (i.e., atrial fibrillation), several million Americans take blood thinners. So this is something that could feasibly turn into a $10 billion-plus per annum revenue opportunity across indications.

In reality, if 611 “makes bank,” it isn't likely to be driven by TKA. These patients only require blood thinners for up to six weeks after surgery, and sometimes, it’s just a daily aspirin that is needed.

That said, TKA is a really good choice for “proof-of-concept” because of limited follow-up, so it’s not a super-expensive and complicated trial. It’s pretty straightforward. The real opportunity is in those long-term indications like atrial fibrillation.

Of course, CRISPR is also still exploring CAR-T applications across autoimmune and oncology indications, with a series of updates expected this year.

“CAR-T in autoimmune diseases” is a newer spin that is being explored by multiple drug developers. As I’ve touched on in previous CRSP articles, the company is a bit late to the game here, although its off-the-shelf approach may offer major manufacturing advantages. That is, if “CAR-T in autoimmune diseases” proves to be fruitful, at all.

Final Words

Although CASGEVY continues to drag CRISPR’s financials, pipeline developments continue to warrant interest in CRSP (maintain “Hold”). That said, with an EV >$3 billion and $300-$400 million in annual cash burn, I’m still a bit cautious. They are competing in very crowded spaces (e.g., VTE prophylaxis, autoimmune conditions), so CRISPR’s therapies really need to "stand out from the crowd." Given the intensity of R&D investments, it really needs a breakthrough to break even.

In any event, I do believe the Factor XI space is one to watch closely moving forward. I think there is a major need for innovation in this space - to improve upon blood thinners like ELIQUIS and COUMADIN. If 611 "checks the boxes" (e.g., extended efficacy, safety) in the TKA trial, I think CRSP could see some serious share price appreciation.

This is also something that would slowly be priced into the stock, giving us an opportunity to reassess when we have actual data.

Standard risks apply (i.e., cash burn, dilution, clinical trial failures, competition, etc.)

data.title: Harbor International Compounders ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Philosophy:

The Harbor International Compounders ETF employs a bottom-up, research-driven process to constructing a portfolio of foreign large-cap companies. The Fund invests primarily in equity securities of at least $5B in market cap of non-U.S. companies that the team believes have the potential to deliver sustainable long-term growth.

Performance

As of 12/31/2025

Average Annual Returns

Ticker CUSIP 3 Months YTD 1 Yr. 3 Yr. 5 Yr. 10 Yr. Since Inception Inception Date Gross Expense Ratio %
Harbor International Compounders ETF (NAV) OSEA 41151J885 3.80% 17.89% 17.89% 12.45% N/A N/A 14.27% 09/07/2022 0.55
Harbor International Compounders ETF (Market) OSEA 41151J885 3.58% 18.49% 18.49% 12.45% N/A N/A 14.33% 09/07/2022 0.55
MSCI All Country World Ex. US (ND) Index 5.05% 32.39% 32.39% 17.33% N/A N/A 17.66% 09/07/2022

Manager Commentary

“With the U.S. now comprising roughly 65% of global equity indexes, historical parallels suggest caution.”

C Worldwide Asset Management

Market in Review

Europe remained characterized by low growth rather than contraction, with the European Central Bank on hold. Japan, by contrast, moved in the opposite direction, as the Bank of Japan continued its cautious policy normalization. China is still struggling with weak domestic demand, although expectations remain for further policy support.

Global equity markets were lifted by the Federal Reserve’s pivot in monetary policy, and early signs of an important market shift have emerged. After a prolonged period of narrow market leadership, there are now indications that the market is broadening beyond a small group of artificial intelligence winners delivering strong performance. It bodes well for active management when companies across a wider range of industries, i.e., a “broader” market, can perform.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor International Compounders ETF (Institutional Class, “ETF”) returned 3.80% (NAV), underperforming the benchmark, the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index, which returned 5.05%.

A large part of the underperformance can be attributed to the ETF’s lack of exposure to Western banks, while having exposure to emerging markets banks and financial services companies. In addition, stock selection within Materials and Consumers Discretionary negatively affected performance. Stock selection within Health Care and lack of exposure to Communication Services was additive to relative results during the period.

Security selection in Financials, Materials, Consumer Discretionary, and Information Technology detracted the most from relative performance, while security selection in Health Care added modest value. Although sector allocation effects are residual to our bottom-up process, they weighed on relative returns this period. Security selection in Information Technology was the largest detractor, while the lack of exposure to Materials contributed positively.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at Active ETFs or by calling 800-422-1050.

Shares are bought and sold at market price not net asset value (NAV). A fund’s NAV is the sum of all its assets less any liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

Contributors & Detractors

Prosus (PROSY), RELX (RELX), and Linde (LIN) detracted the most. Although Prosus reported strong half-year results for fiscal year 2026, management flagged risks from rapid AI-driven changes in consumer behavior and intensifying competition that could pressure future growth.

RELX reported solid revenue and earnings growth for the first nine months of 2025. During the quarter, we trimmed our position in Linde, which was purely a portfolio rebalancing and funding move for our purchase of Tesco. Since our initial investment in Linde, it has slightly outperformed the ACWI ex-US. We still believe it holds a strong business in an attractive industry and has a highly defensive and predictable earnings stream via long-term contracts.

Key contributors to performance in the quarter were AstraZeneca and SSE. AstraZeneca reported a strong third quarter, exceeding consensus on revenue, earnings before interest and taxes, and earnings per share ("EPS"), driven by oncology, while disciplined selling, general, and administrative expenses supported earnings growth. Oral oncology momentum is expected to continue into 2026, with upcoming launches requiring limited incremental investment. Management reaffirmed confidence in achieving a mid-30% margin next year, and U.S. $80bn in revenue by 2030, despite new U.S. pricing policies.

SSE shares rose following the release of fiscal year 2030 ("FY30") strategic guidance, which addressed concerns around funding its £33bn capital expenditure plan. Of this, £21bn is expected to be funded through operating cash flow, with most spending weighted to the latter part of the decade. Management reaffirmed its commitment to maintaining net debt/earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization below 4.5x FY30 guidance implying a 9%–11% EPS compound annual growth rate, alongside a potential dividend yield of approximately 3.5%.

Buys & Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Contemporary Amperex Technology (CTATF), MercadoLibre (MELI), Rheinmetall (RNMBF), Tesco (TSCO), and added further to Prosus.

Contemporary Amperex Technology raises our exposure to Chinese innovation and battery technology, which we believe will have a strong future. It is the world's largest lithium-ion battery producer, spanning cell manufacturing, battery packs, recycling, and next-generation chemistries. Its competitive advantage lies in unmatched scale, vertical integration, cost leadership, and deep partnerships with global automakers. China's investment environment has also become more stable and supportive of advanced manufacturing champions, further strengthening the company's long-term positioning.

Mercado Libre offers exposure to digital consumption and financial inclusion in a structurally underpenetrated region. E-commerce penetration remains low (14% of retail vs. 44% in China), while access to financial services is limited. Mercado Libre operates a "super-app," integrating commerce, payments, credit, logistics, and advertising, creating a data-rich ecosystem well positioned for AI-driven personalization and monetization.

Rheinmetall is emerging as Europe's de facto defense systems integrator, with a broad portfolio spanning ammunition, vehicles, sensors, and drones. Europe's rearmament, structurally higher ammunition demand, and a shift toward assured-supply procurement favor scale players with captive intellectual property and domestic capacity. Rheinmetall benefits from unmatched NATO-standard ammunition capacity, strong political alignment with Germany, and returns are improving as its product mix shifts to ammunition and high-margin systems, with rising visibility via multiyear framework contracts and cost-plus elements that help preserve downside.

We sold our positions in Novo Nordisk (NVO), Atlas Copco (ATLKY), SMC (SMCAY), Ferguson (FERG), and Diageo (DEO).

We exited our Novo Nordisk position after a long and partly successful journey since our initial purchase in November 2011. Although we meaningfully reduced the position when it was at much higher levels, we stayed invested for too long. We sold it due to weakening confidence in pricing, competition, management and board changes, and the innovation pipeline. Our latest engagement suggests limited earnings growth through 2026 and increased downside risk, prompting capital reallocation.

While Atlas Copco is a high-quality compounding industrial, headwinds, tariffs, foreign exchange pressure, and elevated valuation may leave the stock treading water for some time.

Sector Overweights & Underweights

Sector weightings are a residual of our bottom-up stock selection process. As of December 31, 2025, the ETF's largest sector overweights were Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care. Compared with the MSCI ACWI ex-US Index, the ETF was overweight Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care, while the largest underweights were Financials, Communication Services, and Energy.

Our sector weightings shifted this quarter due to recent portfolio changes. Exposure to Industrials was reduced by 2.2%, while exposure to Consumer Discretionary increased by 2.8%. These changes were driven by bottom-up stock selection rather than a top-down view of sector attractiveness.

Outlook

Equity markets have been in a phase where the magnitude of growth has been rewarded over the sustainability of growth. Speculative behavior, index-driven flows, and concentration in a narrow set of U.S. growth stocks have led to valuation distortions and challenged active managers, including ourselves. While beating the market is difficult, we believe it remains achievable with discipline and a long-term horizon. Importantly, early signs suggest a meaningful shift in market dynamics is underway. We identify four factors of change that point to a renewed focus on fundamentals and structural growth.

First, the revival of quality stocks. After several years of derating, companies with strong balance sheets, high return on capital, durable margins, and sustainable business models are now trading at reasonable valuations. Performance in late 2025 indicates investors are beginning to rediscover long-term quality as an anchor in portfolios.

Second, the broadening of the AI theme. The initial AI boom has been narrow and capital-intensive, exposing concentration and execution risks. We expect the next phase to be broader, more realistic, and global – benefiting a wider range of companies across Europe and Asia, particularly those enabling the physical and energy infrastructure required for AI.

Third, the rise of China and India. Together representing one-third of the world's population, both countries are rapidly moving up the value chain, prioritizing technology, manufacturing, and self-sufficiency. Despite their growing economic and technological importance, they remain significantly underrepresented in global equity benchmarks – an imbalance we expect to correct over time.

Fourth, peak U.S. market dominance. With the U.S. now comprising roughly 65% of global equity indexes, historical parallels suggest caution. Indexation risks reinforcing extremes, as seen previously with Japan.

Looking at 2026, we expect these four forces to drive a market reorientation toward fundamentals, diversification, and sustainable earnings growth. We believe it will be a year of realization that earnings and quality matter, with attractively priced, high-quality companies outside of the U.S. continuing to offer strong alpha potential.

data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For February
data.description:
This monthly article series offers a top-down view of the consumer staples sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), and Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value And Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, and ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: C.PR.R: A 6.25% Preferred Stock IPO From Citigroup
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

With this, we continue our series of articles dedicated to newly listed fixed-income securities on the exchange. This time, our attention will be focused on Citigroup's (C) recent addition - the 6.250% Depositary Shares Noncumulative Preferred Stock, Series II (C.PR.R). As usual for this type of IPO analysis, we'll begin by going through the issuer's fundamentals to figure out whether we want exposure to its preferred equity in the first place. Once this is done, we'll continue with the sector's peer comparison to assess the investment qualities of C-R in a relative value analysis

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information regarding the new preferred stock. Here, we will focus on only the metrics that are most important for our analysis.

C-R pays a 6.25% annual non-cumulative dividend in $0.3906 quarterly distributions. Dividends on the new preferred shares will accrue from February 3, 2026, starting distributions on May 15, 2026. This means that the first dividend payment will be irregular in size and amount to approximately $0.4427. The preferred distributions are eligible for the preferential income tax rate. Citigroup's gross proceeds from the new security are $800 million, from a total of 32 million depositary shares issued with a liquidation preference of $25 each. The new preferred stock issue received a credit score of Ba1 from Moody's and BB+ from S&P at the time of its initial public offering. C-R has no stated maturity date and may be subject to early redemption by Citigroup on or after February 15, 2031.

As the issue was recently listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, C-R is priced at $25.29 with a current yield of 6.18%, and the yield to call is determined to be 6.16% using the XIRR calculation:

This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

The Company

Founded in 1812 and headquartered in New York, Citigroup Inc. is a global diversified financial services holding company, part of the S&P 500, and also part of the US Systematically Important Banks list, commonly known as "Too Big to Fail." It provides its customers with a wide variety of financial services through its subsidiaries: Services, Markets, Banking, Wealth, and U.S. Personal Banking.

Company Performance

The daily chart below shows the market evaluation of the common stock of Citigroup over the past ten years:

We are going to compare the total return performance over the past ten years of C to that of the general market, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE), as a representative for the banking sector.

The chart above shows that Citi is following the same general trend as the US Banking sector. In the past year, its total return performance, due to a large price appreciation, has deviated much on the upside, compared to KBE's. Currently, Citi's 10-year total return has reached a higher value than the S&P 500's.

As for the expected future performance of the company, most of the Wall Street analysts are still bullish with a "Buy" rating, while the majority of SA analysts and the Quant Rating are more cautious in their expectations of a rally continuation, scoring the company with the neutral "Hold."

A direct comparison with Citigroup's direct peers - the largest US diversified banks shows, however, that its total return is not something out of the ordinary, currently placed around the average for this set. Over the past ten years, (GS) and (JPM) have more than twice the total return of C, with GS having the same magnitude of surge in the past twelve months as C:

Capital Structure

Below is the capital structure of Citigroup as of the fourth quarter of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

Citigroup has long-term debt plus short-term borrowings of approximately $335.8 billion, senior to the preferred equity of $20.1 billion. The new preferred stock C-R will increase the preferred equity by at least $0.8 billion (or more if the overallotment is exercised). However, in February 2026, Citigroup announced full redemption of its Series X Preferred Stock with a $2.3 billion liquidation preference and the issuance of the Series JJ Preferred Stock with a $1 billion liquidation preference. This means that the preferred equity will come to around $19.6 billion. At the lowest position of the capital structure is the common equity of approximately $192.1 billion, providing a common/preferred stock ratio of close to ten.

As of the end of 2025, Citigroup has a CET1 Capital Ratio of 13.2% under the Basel III Standardized Approach, a healthy 160 basis points above the 11.6% regulatory requirement.

Dividends

Citigroup has a history of an impressive 14 years of uninterrupted dividend payments, the last three of which have been increasing ones:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to C's shareholders amounts to $2.36 in quarterly payments. With the last closing price of the stock at $110.86, we can calculate a 2.13% dividend yield. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

Citigroup has approximately 1.77 billion common shares outstanding and a $2.40 forward estimated annual payment per share. We can calculate approximately 4.25 billion forward annualized distributions for the common stockholders. The TTM distribution to Citi's preferred shareholders amounts to approximately $1.1 billion. The redemption of the Series X preferred stock will decrease this value by approximately $89 million, and the dividends on C-R will increase it by $50 million, and the Series JJ will require another $65 million. This means that the expected preferred dividend in the next twelve months will be approximately $1.13 billion if there are no further changes in the company's preferred equity. This means that the common shares' distribution covers close to four times the preferred shares' dividend on an annual basis. The cushion is good enough for the preferred shareholders to feel reasonably assured that their dividends are well covered for the foreseeable future.

Credit Rating

Fitch Ratings - New York - 15 Aug 2025: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Citigroup Inc.'s (Citi) Long- and Short-Term Issuer Default Ratings (IDRs) at 'A' and 'F1', respectively, and has affirmed Citi's Viability Rating (VR) at 'a'. The Rating Outlook is Stable. Fitch has also affirmed the IDRs of related entities Citibank, N.A (CBNA), Citibank Canada, Citibank Europe Plc, Citigroup Global Markets Holdings Inc., Citigroup Global Markets Limited, and Citigroup Global Markets, Inc.

.....

The ratings reflect progress in Citi's regulatory remediation, as well as improvements to its business profile. Upward assessments across several key rating drivers provide Citi with greater headroom within its current rating.

This is the beginning of the recent rating action commentary with which Fitch has affirmed the "A" long-term issuer default rating with a Stable Outlook of Citigroup.

Despite some ups and downs in the Outlook, the long-term IDR of Citigroup has been unchanged by Fitch since December 2011, when it was downgraded from the 'A+' value before that:

Citigroup's Debt

Citigroup has a variety of OTC-traded bond issues, both senior and subordinated. As C-R is a perpetuity, we'd like to compare it with the issues with the longest possible duration. In this case, this will be the '98, A3-rated by Moody's issue. With four notches higher than C-R's Ba1 credit quality, it is priced with a YTW of 5.75%. Compared to this yield, the current yield of 6.18% is way too low, making the new preferred stock overvalued. It looks overvalued even compared with the '43 - '46 A3-rated debt issues with yields to worst of 5.4-5.5%.

The majority of the long-term debt issues of Citi are with maturities of approximately 20 years, priced with yields of approximately 80 basis points higher than the 20-year treasuries. Looking at the Single-A-rated US corporate-issued debt OAS below, we can assess that Citigroup's long-term debt is somewhat fairly priced to the debt of peers of comparable credit quality.

Citigroup's Listed Securities

Besides the new issue, Citigroup has one more listed security - the Citigroup Capital XIII, 7.875% Fixed/Floating TruPS Trust Preferred Securities (C.PR.N). C-N and C-R are quite different types of security. The Trust issuing C-N is funded by junior subordinated debt of Citigroup, so C-N is legally a junior subordinated debt and lies higher than C-R in the capital structure of Citi. This is reflected in its credit quality, too - Baa3 by Moody's and BBB- by Fitch. Its distributions are not dividends but interest, being derived from the underlying in the Trust debt securities. As such, these are not eligible for the preferential tax income.

Peer Comparison

As C-R is priced close to par, I'll compare it with the bank-issued preferred stocks priced above par and trading before their call dates by the YTC metric and to the fixed-rate ones priced below par by the CY. For the comparisons, I've filtered only the preferreds comparable to C-R in credit quality.

And the metrics:

And the current yield comparison:

With the relevant metrics:

Whether one chooses to evaluate C-R by its YTC or CY metrics, the security shines with nothing among its direct peers. C-R is offering yields that we can currently find in fixed-income securities, with a few notches higher credit quality. I find the new preferred of Citigroup unattractive at its current pricing.

Use of Proceeds

We expect to use the net proceeds from the sale of the depositary shares representing interests in the Preferred Stock for general corporate purposes, which may include the partial or full redemption of outstanding shares of Citigroup preferred stock and related depositary shares, as applicable, and repurchases and redemptions of other outstanding securities of Citigroup and its subsidiaries, including Citigroup common stock. This prospectus supplement does not constitute a notice of redemption with respect to any outstanding series of Citigroup preferred stock nor an obligation to issue a notice of redemption for any outstanding series of Citigroup preferred stock. Any such notice, if given, will only be given in accordance with the provisions of such outstanding series of preferred stock, as applicable.

Source: C-R Prospectus

Redemption Considerations

The Preferred Stock is perpetual and has no maturity date. Citigroup may redeem the Preferred Stock in whole at any time or in part, from time to time, on any dividend payment date on or after February 15, 2031 or in whole but not in part at any time within 90 days following a Regulatory Capital Event (as defined on page S-16), in each case at a cash redemption price equal to $25,000 per share of Preferred Stock (equivalent to $25 per depositary share) plus any declared and unpaid dividends and without accumulation of any undeclared dividends to, but excluding, the redemption date.

....

Redemption of the Preferred Stock will be subject to receipt of any required prior approval of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York (the “Federal Reserve”), or any successor appropriate federal banking agency. Under the Federal Reserve’s current capital rules, Citigroup would need prior regulatory approval to redeem the Preferred Stock. Neither the holders of the Preferred Stock nor the holders of the depositary shares will have the right to require redemption.

Source: C-R Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face value outstanding of the new preferred stock C-R of $800 million makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

The capital structure of Citigroup is stable, well within the regulatory requirements. The dividend coverage of the preferred equity is adequate, and the debt of the company does not indicate particular concerns among the institutional investors. However, I find the yield C-R is offering unattractive. Currently, there are plenty of fixed-income securities with higher credit quality, comparable or higher yields, and offering a better appreciation potential on a downshift in long-term yields.

The fund's gross expense ratio is 0.88%. The fund's net expense ratio is 0.67%. The manager has contractually committed, through November 30, 2026, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund's total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.67% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund's returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Quarterly review

  • The Large Cap Core Fund underperformed the S&P 500 Index benchmark during the fourth quarter.
  • For the three-month period, industrials, information technology (IT), and energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Financials, real estate, and utilities contributed to relative performance.

Market review

The S&P 500 Index advanced 17.88% during 2025, notching a third consecutive year above 15% performance for the first time since 2021. U.S. equities extended their gains for a third consecutive quarter, with the S&P 500 Index rising 2.66% and setting multiple record closing highs during the fourth quarter. U.S. small- and mid-cap stocks were also positive during the quarter, with the Russell 2000 Index gaining 2.19% and the Russell 2500 Index returning 2.22%. The broadening of market returns was supported by solid corporate earnings, continued enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI), a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), easing trade tensions, and resilient consumer sentiment. International equity markets fared even better, with the MSCI World Index ex USA (NET) rising 5.05% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (NET) returning 4.73%. U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, value outperformed growth, and West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices fell for a fourth straight quarter, ending the quarter down 9.1%.

After a record-long federal government shutdown that canceled October's Consumer Price Index (CPI) release, November's report unexpectedly showed a sharp decline in inflation. Headline U.S. inflation eased to +2.7% year over year, while core CPI fell to +2.6%, marking the slowest pace of price growth in nearly five years. Price growth decelerated across most consumer categories, offsetting lingering price pressures from energy, food, and shelter. While the incomplete November data warrants caution until December's figures are available, the report suggested continued progress toward the Fed's 2% annualized inflation target. Despite stabilizing home prices and growing inventory, the U.S. housing market remained stagnant due to affordability constraints, elevated mortgage rates, and slowing job growth.

In December, the U.S. Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), to a new target range of 3.50–3.75%, marking its third consecutive 25-bp rate cut. The move reflected signs of slowing U.S. economic growth and emerging labor market risks. However, the Fed signaled a pause, noting it will assess “the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range.” Current projections call for only one additional 25-bp rate cut for 2026, which would lower the federal funds rate to approximately 3.40% by the end of 2026. The Fed lifted its 2026 U.S. real gross domestic product growth forecast to 2.3% from 1.8%, while keeping its unemployment projection unchanged at 4.4%. Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index inflation is now expected to ease to 3.0% in 2025 and then decline further to 2.5% in 2026 and 2.1% in 2027.

Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

The Large Cap Core Fund returned 0.76% for the quarter while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.66%. The fund's underperformance was attributed primarily to negative stock selection within the industrials, IT, and energy sectors. Positive stock selection within the real estate, financials, and consumer staples sectors contributed to performance.

A leading contributor for the quarter was Twilio. Twilio is a leading provider in the growing communications platform-as-a-service market offering a robust cloud-based platform and proprietary application programming interfaces that enable developers to integrate voice, video, text, and email capabilities into their own software applications. Key cloud communication clients include Lyft, American Red Cross, Airbnb, Salesforce, and Twitter. Twilio delivered strong third quarter results with revenue growth accelerating 15% year over year on broad-based strength across customer segments, products, and geographies. Active customer accounts expanded to 392,000, driven by notable growth in the messaging, voice, voice AI, and verify segments. Investors were further encouraged as management raised fiscal-year 2025 guidance for revenue, profitability, and free cash flow while also announcing the acquisition of Styxch, an identity and authentication platform for AI agents. Twilio's market leadership, developer-centric model, operational discipline, and scalable product suite leave the company well positioned amid an evolving cloud and AI-driven engagement landscape.

Netflix, Inc., the world's leading streaming platform serving nearly 300 million subscribers across 190 countries, was a detractor for the quarter. The company's content is available on a wide range of devices, including PCs, TVs, gaming consoles, and smartphones. Netflix reported mixed third-quarter results with revenue growth meeting expectations and advertising revenue reaching a record high. However, investors were disappointed as operating margins and earnings due fell short due to a tax dispute in Brazil.

Performance was supported by robust ad sales, steady subscriber growth, strong user engagement, and recent price increases. Despite these positives, the stock came under pressure as management issued cautious fourth quarter 2025 revenue guidance and investors weighed potential costs and regulatory hurdles tied to Netflix's proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery. Looking ahead, Netflix is poised to benefit from the global proliferation of internet-connected devices and preference for on-demand video. Netflix's growth outlook remains strong, supported by a dual revenue model of subscriptions and a rapidly scaling ad-supported tier. Subscriber momentum continues from password-sharing crackdowns and strategic price increases, while engagement is reinforced by hit content and expansion into live events such as the NFL, WWE, and boxing. Strong financials, including rising operating margins, significant free cash flow, and aggressive share buyback, underscore Netflix's ability to return capital while funding growth. Coupled with AI-driven ad innovations and a premium content slate, Netflix remains well positioned for sustained revenue acceleration and margin expansion.

Fund positioning and outlook

As we enter 2026, U.S. equity markets are navigating a fine line between bullish optimism and growing investor caution. U.S. stocks remain near record highs, supported by resilient corporate earnings, sustained momentum in AI innovation, and a more accommodative Fed. Three rate cuts in 2025 and expectations for additional easing in 2026 have bolstered valuations, particularly in growth-oriented sectors. Despite this positive backdrop, the elevated valuations of mega-cap technology stocks leave little margin for error. Investors remain vigilant about persistent inflation, tariff risks, margin pressures, and signs of labor market weakness. AI-driven productivity gains are expected to broaden, but market volatility could rise sharply if AI momentum falters and investors start demanding greater compensation for risk.

Despite a strong rebound since April's "Liberation Day" sell-off, U.S. stocks continued to trail major global indexes, partly due to a weaker dollar. If trade negotiations progress and tariffs stabilize, lower rates and accelerating AI-driven productivity should enable U.S. equities to regain leadership versus international markets. A reduction in policy uncertainty could support a dollar recovery and refocus investor attention on the long-term potential of AI-led growth. While trade policy remains in the hands of President Trump, we suspect markets may act as a guardrail again if tariff tensions reescalate.

In May of 2025, Moody's downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing rising deficits from higher debt service costs and new spending initiatives. Just weeks later, Congress passed the One Big Beautiful Bill, a sweeping package of trillions in spending and tax cuts that reignited concerns about ballooning fiscal deficits and the long-term sustainability of the U.S. national debt. The U.S. federal deficit, which briefly narrowed to $1.4 trillion in 2022, is now projected to exceed $1.9 trillion in the 2025 fiscal year, which is roughly 6% of GDP. This deterioration comes as unemployment hovers near 4% and the debt-to-GDP ratio approaches 125%, underscoring mounting fiscal pressures despite a relatively strong labor market.

While U.S. inflation is expected to ease in 2026, reaching the Fed's 2% target will likely take longer than anticipated. Trade policy uncertainty has led businesses and consumers to adopt a "wait-and-see" approach. Tariffs are unlikely to cause a U.S. recession if their revenue can be effectively reinvested into the economy, but tariffs may contribute to slower GDP growth and place upward pressure on core CPI inflation. Loose fiscal policy may be partially offset by government job cuts and the Fed's data-dependent approach to rate adjustments. A further moderation in service prices and wage growth may be necessary to move inflation closer to the Fed's target. Persistent inflation combined with solid U.S. GDP growth is expected to limit the Fed to just one 25-bp cut in 2026. In the meantime, governments, corporations, investors, and households will continue adapting to an environment of elevated interest rates.

Consensus estimates currently project 15% earnings growth for the S&P 500 Index in fiscal-year 2026, with the index trading at a forward P/E of approximately 23 times. While this is not necessarily a bubble valuation level, the S&P 500 Index's market capitalization has rarely been this high as a share of global GDP. Despite mega-cap technology stocks leading once again in 2025, equity returns broadened out relative to 2023 and 2024, and investors are increasingly being rewarded for diversifying further down the market-cap spectrum. U.S. small- and mid-cap stocks remain attractively valued relative to large caps, offering compelling upside potential amid broader market uncertainty. We expect this market environment to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did throughout 2025.

Given the economic, geopolitical, and market backdrop, we anticipate volatile and range-bound market conditions in the near term. We will neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are cautious, we will look to take advantage of market volatility to invest in companies with strong fundamentals at reasonable valuations. As we monitor the macroeconomic environment, we will continue to diligently focus on company fundamentals and disciplined portfolio risk management. From a sector perspective, the portfolio is currently overweight health care, energy, and real estate while it is underweight consumer discretionary, utilities, and financials.

It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI calculates the cost to purchase a fixed basket of goods to determine how much inflation is occurring in the broad economy. The CPI uses a base year and indexes the current year's prices, according to the base year's values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Core Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, but excludes food and energy. Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Price/earnings (P/E) is the price of a share of a stock divided by earnings per share, usually calculated using the latest year's earnings.

Definition of terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. data.title: NVDU As AI Stocks Dip
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) dominates AI hardware with GPUs essential for AI model training. For non-technical investors like me, this means NVDA produces very sophisticated computer chips that are necessary to support artificial intelligence, given its massive hardware power and a near-monopoly in the software ecosystem.

According to NVDA’s CEO, Jensen Huang, “The ChatGPT moment for physical AI is here when machines begin to understand, reason, and act in the real world.” He also warned that “You won’t lose your job to AI; you’ll lose your job to someone who uses AI.”

After reaching a record high in October 2025, NVDA shares have pulled back and consolidated. Over the past weeks, weakness in the technology sector has raised concerns that NVDA shares could be overdue for a correction. However, the company’s leadership role and virtual monopoly increase the odds that higher highs will continue. The Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NVDU) could be a useful product ahead of the company’s announcement of earnings later this month.

Nvidia Remains the Most Valuable Company in the World

NVDA continues to lead the world in market capitalization.

The chart shows that the top 10 companies by market capitalization all had market caps over $1.5 trillion. NVDA leads the pack with a value three times that of $4.45 trillion, $691 billion more than second-place Apple (AAPL).

AI Supports a Higher NVDA Share Price

NDVA’s company profile states:

In the technology sector, artificial intelligence is the holy grail. NVDA produces the chips supporting AI. Some of the highlights of NVDA’s AI-driven focus are:

The Compute & Networking segment includes its Data Centre accelerated computing platforms, artificial intelligence solutions, and software.

Quadro/Nvidia RTX GPUs include Omniverse software for building and operating AI and digital twin applications. It also provides customized agentic solutions, designed in collaboration with NVDA, to accelerate enterprise AI adoption.

The company also has a strategic partnership with Siemens Aktiengesellschaft (SIEGY) to develop industrial AI solutions for AI-driven innovation in every industry and industrial workflow.

AI-related Stocks Have Corrected

Many AI-related stocks have experienced downside corrections over the past weeks. NVDA’s correction began in late October 2025 at its all-time high of $212.19 per share.

The chart shows that NVDA shares fell 20% from the October 29, 2025, high of $212.19 to the November 25 low of $169.55 per share. Meanwhile, in a bullish sign for the stock, NVDA has made higher lows on December 17, 2025, and most recently on February 5, 2026. At the $182.81 level on February 13, the stock was trading below the midpoint of the October through November 2025 trading range.

NVDA Will Report Earnings in Late February- a $5 Trillion Market Cap Could Be on the Horizon

NVDA will report earnings on February 25, 2026:

The chart shows that NVDA beat EPS and revenue forecasts in November, and analysts have raised their expectations ahead of the February 25 report.

Meanwhile, NVDA has a history of beating analyst forecasts on earnings reports:

As the chart shows, the company will need to continue producing substantial revenue and earnings growth to keep pace with future consensus forecasts.

NVDA’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades continue to earn a failing grade in valuation due to its sky-high price-to-earnings ratio of nearly 39 times earnings. However, profitability consistently earns the highest possible A+, while growth prospects receive an A. The stock’s lethargic price action over the past months earns a B grade in momentum, while NVDA earns a B+ grade in the revisions category.

NVDA’s next line in the sand comes at the end of this month when it releases its earnings report.

NVDU Is a Short-term Trading Vehicle That Turbocharges NVDA Share Performance

NVDA is a market leader, and the path of least resistance of its stock leads other technology and AI companies. After the recent downside correction, another forecast-beating earnings report could flip the script for another leg up in AI and technology stocks.

The Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF is a leveraged product that turbocharges the price action in NVDA shares. At $110.15 per share, NVDU had over $583 million in assets under management. NVDU trades an average of over 469,000 shares per day and charges a 0.97% management fee. According to Seeking Alpha’s metrics, NVDU’s dividend yield is 6.06%.

NVDA shares rose 145% from the April 2025 low of $86.62 to the October 2025 high of $212.19 per share before correcting 20% to the November 2025 $169.55 per share low.

Over the same period, the leveraged NVDU ETF rose 408% from the April 2025 low of $32.63 to the October 2025 high of $165.78 per share, then corrected 41.3% to the February 2026 low of $97.39 per share.

NVDU’s leverage, creating time decay, earns a failing grade in the risk category. The bearish trend and high management fee receive D- grades, while the daily volume and assets under management earn an A- in the liquidity category. If Seeking Alpha’s dividend metric is correct, the D in the dividend category is the only questionable grade.

NVDU is a tool suitable only for short-term risk positions on the long side of NVDA shares. When approaching leveraged NVDA exposure with NVDU, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to oversized losses. Moreover, stable NVDA share prices will likely cause NVDU to decline, as time decay erodes its value. The swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risks. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as NVDU.

Meanwhile, with earnings on the horizon, NVDA’s history of beating the forecasts, and the recent correction in AI and technology-related stocks, NVDU could be a valuable tool to magnify NVDA’s stock performance over the coming weeks if another earnings beat pushes the stock higher.

data.title: PGIM Strategic Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • With 2026 underway, a few factors are readily apparent across the global fixed income markets: the slow-going bull market remains in the sweet spot; attractive yield levels should continue to accrue into solid returns over the intermediate to longer term; and the unusual geopolitical backdrop and asynchronous central bank cycles should continue to create opportunities to add value through active management.
  • Although events like Q1's tariff unveiling and accompanying market swoon created bumps along the way, the ongoing economic expansion with moderate growth and inflation has kept yields generally high and range bound—an environment where the highest-yielding sectors continued to post the highest returns.
  • Although excess returns from spread products were once again positive in 2025—as they have been throughout the three years of the bull market—they were more muted. Spreads are narrower, and the bulk of the capital gains potential from narrowing spreads is well behind us at this point of the cycle.
  • Thanks to the positive yield curve and a slight drop in yields, Treasuries finally outperformed cash and joined the bull market last year. Two prevailing themes from late 2025—renewed divergence across global monetary policy rates and the repricing of term premia—will likely remain at play within developed market rate complexes with 2026 underway. So, while we expect a smaller return contribution from spread product going forward, long-term fixed income should pick up a tailwind from the yield advantage and roll down benefits provided by the newly positive yield curves.
  • In the U.S., the Fed's 25 bp rate cut in December carried a dovish tone, leading to a bull steepening along the curve with only slight movement in the 10-year yield. The narrow move on the 10-year underscored its prevailing low-volatility, range-bound conditions throughout 2025. The low-volatility conditions in the U.S. were further amplified by the selloff across other developed market yield curves late last year. Indeed, at the front of the curve, the 2-year U.S. yield outperformed its global counterparts by a wide margin as the end of several policy easing cycles came into sight.
  • Over the coming 12 months, our “Muddle Through” base case for the U.S. sees further weakening in the labor market against a backdrop of stubbornly high inflation between 2.5- 3.0%. The Fed, torn between the conflicting signals of its dual mandate, will likely chart a patient path to the estimated neutral range of monetary policy (i.e., 3.0-3.25%) in 2026. While not our base case, once a new Fed chair is confirmed, the potential exists for the policy rate to decline towards a sub-neutral range of 2.0-2.5% in the second half of 2026.
  • In Europe, our base case also anticipates near-trend growth (~1.2- 1.4%) as rising real incomes and fiscal support underpin the consumer, while exports and investment remain challenged. A mild, but extended, period where inflation undershoots the ECB's 2% target may incentivize the ECB to implement another 25 bps rate cut to bring the deposit rate to the lower side of neutral at 1.75%.
  • While we can hardly be certain as to what this year will bring, our a priori is more of the same: high and range bound yields look set to persist, allowing this slow-go bull market to continue, where returns accrue not by a quick drop in yields and rise in prices, but rather thanks to the ongoing earning of yield itself.
  • Additionally, albeit by thinner margins and with more idiosyncratic risk, spread product looks set to deliver positive, but perhaps narrower, excess returns. Additionally, newly positive yield curves should give long-term fixed income a performance advantage relative to cash in the quarters ahead.
  • We remain guardedly optimistic on entry into what stands to be year four of the “yield is destiny” bond bull market, where high and range bound yields result in solid returns, and confusion creates ample opportunities for adding value through active management. Given a wide spectrum of risks and outcomes, vigilance remains in order.
