PGIM Strategic Bond Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, and 5-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Multi-Sector Income Fund category were: 115/377, 240/351, and 212/305, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2025: 113/387, 147/370, 234/329, and 45/224, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

4Q 2025 Performance

  • The Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Aggregate Bond Index, on a gross basis.
  • Overall security selection was the largest contributor to performance during the period, with selection in U.S. investment grade corporates, U.S. high yield corporates, non-agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and European bank loans contributing the most. This was partially offset by selection in GBP high yield corporates, AA collateralized loan obligations (s), emerging-market high yield, and U.S. bank loans, which detracted the most.
  • Overall sector allocation also contributed to performance, with overweights to the emerging-market high yield, AA, and non-agency CMBS AA-and-below sectors, along with an underweight to the U.S. investment grade corporate sector contributing the most. This was partially offset by overweights to the U.S. high yield corporate and GBP high yield corporate sectors, along with an underweight to the MBS sector, which detracted.
  • Within credit, positioning in media & entertainment, gaming/lodging/leisure, and healthcare & pharmaceuticals, contributed the most to results. Positioning in telecom and cable & satellite detracted.
  • From a market perspective, having more risk in the Fund, on average, than the relevant benchmark over the period contributed to performance. In aggregate, the Fund's duration and yield curve positioning contributed to performance as front-end yields on the U.S. Treasury yields declined.

Top-Down Macro Positioning

Duration (Current: 4.4 yrs, Previous Quarter: 4.5 yrs)

  • We are inclined to express our duration views through the options markets. Q4's low-volatility trajectory is unlikely to continue in 2026. Our economic scenarios have the widest and thickest tails in recent memory, yet the markets have coalesced around a very narrow distribution of outcomes. This seems inconsistent and, as a result, we expect volatility to move higher in 2026.
  • Notably, in the U.S., Chair Powell's term will conclude in May 2026. However, the tension regarding the Fed likely raises the hurdle for the next Fed Chair to establish monetary-policy credibility and to sustain the perception of independence.
  • At this point, the market is pricing in a Fed funds rate of 3.0% by the end of 2026. A push into accommodative territory is likely to require a combination of a more dovish Fed voting slate and/or a significant slowing in growth and/or inflation. While not our base case, once a new Fed chair is confirmed, the potential exists for the policy rate to decline towards a sub-neutral range of 2.0-2.5% in the second half of 2026.

Curve Positioning

  • The 2022 selloff left yields at respectable levels—which remains largely intact—with an unusual net result: a potentially long-lived bull market primarily driven by the accretion of income, rather than a short bull market fueled by a quick drop in yields. Long-term fixed income should pick up a tailwind from the yield advantage and roll down benefits provided by newly positive yield curves. In the U.S., the positive yield curve and range-bound market highlight two themes: 1) bonds should outperform cash on yield; and 2) swings in sentiment should lift duration management as a tactical alpha driver.
  • Steeper yield curves should remain prominent globally. The long end of developed-market sovereign curves have become more sensitive to fiscal news. Geopolitical rivalry is expensive. Record defense budgets, expanding industrial policy, and rising populism will compel major economies to run historically high deficits. Accompanying demands for low monetary policy rates erodes central banks' inflation-fighting credibility. Repriced term premia will increasingly determine the level of long-term rates.
  • In the EA, the top down growth story will be driven primarily by strong government support, both in spending and investment, masking weak private sector dynamism. Our base case is that quarterly GDP growth will gently accelerate, reflecting inertia in the periphery and stronger activity in France and Germany on the back of higher fiscal spending.

Bottom-Up Sector Positioning

  • In Q4, credit products generally posted positive total returns, with spreads for investment grade corporates remaining tight and high yield spreads unchanged, amid strong technicals and stable fundamentals. Securitized credit spreads were mixed, with high-quality commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) tightening and collateralized loan obligations (s) widening over the quarter. In addition, emerging-market hard currency, local rates, and corporates posted strong total returns as spreads tightened.
  • The current environment continues to create distortions in the interest rate, sector, issuer, and currency realms. This results in risks, but also opportunities to add value through active management across the fixed income asset spectrum.
  • The trend of compressing credit spreads should generally continue, albeit more modestly given the tight spread levels. These conditions describe “a carry market” if there ever was one. In terms of positioning, we see sovereign bonds as more attractive than credit. Consequently, we continue to advocate for lighter risk and are focused on carry-type instruments.

Corporate Credit

Investment-Grade Corporates (Current: 10.7%, Previous Quarter 10.1%)

  • A resilient economy in 2025 and attractive yields made for a roughly unchanged Index and carry-like returns in the U.S. IG market. Other contributing factors included robust inflows, manageable gross and net issuance, and generally stable fundamentals. Heading into 2026, leverage has been stable, and profit margins are near historical highs.
  • We expect the economy and yields to remain favorable, with strong earnings growth from the S&P 500. That stated, the very strong technical picture from 2025 is expected to weaken with increased gross and net supply, arising from growing capex, especially from the Tech sector. In addition, the backdrop is favorable for M&A and this corporate behavior could weaken credit fundamentals.
  • Broader-based earnings growth is expected. Unlike in prior years, growth for 2026 will not be concentrated in the “Mag 7.” In fact, EPS is expected to grow ~12% YoY in 2026, with slightly more than half of that growth coming from the S&P 493.
  • Spreads on the U.S. IG Index remain tight, credit curves are flat, and BBBs are tight to As. Increased supply—attributed to a pickup in M&A and capex activity—could cause credit curves to steepen in 2026. While it is uncertain how aggressive management teams will be with balance sheets, more credit dispersion is expected. Overall, we believe spreads on the Index will likely trade within a 90-95 bps range.
  • Between $1.8- $2.2 trillion in gross issuance and $850-$950 billion in net issuance is anticipated in 2026. Most of the supply will come from higher-quality, non-financial issuers in technology (e.g., hyperscalers), utilities (related to the AI buildout), pipelines, and pharmaceuticals (tariff-related).
  • In terms of position, we favor banks, technology, and select utilities. We are underweight life insurance, chemicals, and pharmaceuticals. Potential Risks include slowing economic growth, fiscal deficits, inflation, Fed policy, geopolitical uncertainty, rate volatility, and corporate event risk.

High Yield Corporates (Current: 17.1%, Previous Quarter 19.3%)

  • We expect U.S. HY-bond spreads to remain range-bound, near historic tights, over the coming months due to strong technicals and robust credit fundamentals. HY spreads entered a late-cycle phase—i.e., an environment of tight spreads and low volatility—that could last throughout much of 2026.
  • While relatively tame “animal spirits” have curtailed primary supply. We expect increased M&A, LBO, and AI-related capex transactions in 2026, which limits any meaningful spread tightening from current levels. That stated, the sector should still generate positive total returns in the coming quarters, particularly as a potential rise in defaults amid re-leveraging and more aggressive behavior could be years away.
  • Solid market technicals should persist beyond Q1, supported by the healthy pace of refinancing, low speculative borrowing, and modest inflows. Further, the continued strength in credit fundamentals provides a buffer against macro uncertainty. However, geopolitical risks remain, while potential overheating from monetary and fiscal stimulus could push spreads wider, and the use of PE dry powder could reignite speculative issuance.
  • In terms of sectors, we are maintaining our overweight to short-duration bonds and continue to reduce our underweight to high-quality issues. Looking at sectors, we are overweight home construction, telecom, and midstream energy, and underweight technology, media & entertainment, and retailers & restaurants. However, recent adjustments include an increase to our overweight in paper & packaging, a further reduction in our overweights to building materials, home construction, and telecom, and an increase in our underweight to technology

Bank Loans (Current: 1.1%, Previous Quarter 1.1%)

  • In 2026, we expect returns to be driven primarily by carry, but tempered by downward pressure from lower base rates, continued repricing transactions, elevated credit concerns, and potential technical pressures if net issuance accelerates. While we do not expect broad-based deterioration in fundamentals, defaults and LMEs should remain elevated with recoveries below historical averages.
  • We continue to favor public BB and high B loans over sponsor-owned, low B and CCC loans as we expect those lower-quality facilities to be challenged by the fundamental backdrop. We believe that deep, fundamental credit research/modeling is increasingly important to credit selection. To that end, avoidance of defaults will likely be the biggest driver of alpha over the next 12-24 months.

SECURITIZED (ABS)

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) (Current: 7.2%, Previous Quarter 7.0%)

  • Valuations for most property types have stabilized, and we expect price appreciation to be flat for the year—likely no V-shaped recovery given long-term rate expectations. Rent growth continues normalizing across property types and may increase as supply pressures abate but will likely stay within range of historical averages.
  • Elevated single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) supply has contributed to attractive spreads, and we see value in high-quality deals with structural protections. In conduit markets, we favor shorter spread duration given the current term-curve profile

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) (Current: 9.9%, Previous Quarter 10.9%)

  • In CLOs, senior tranches continue to offer attractive relative value compared to many fixed income asset classes, while selectively adding higher-quality mezzanine tranches. With CLO spreads still inside of their long-term averages, but wide of 2025 tights, we expect mixed valuations in the near term. This should create entry points at wider levels.
  • CLO fundamentals are improving—supported by growth in revenue and earnings as well as generally positive outlooks—but tail risks remain, i.e., more than 10% of underlying collateral features stressed interest-coverage ratios (below 1.5x).

Other ABS (Current: 1.3%, Previous Quarter 1.4%)

  • In asset-backed securities (ABS), the consumer remains stretched—especially the non-prime cohort—as they take on more debt and elevated default rates continue in seasoned ABS trusts. Considering the spread compression globally and pronounced credit tiering, we’re positioned toward top-tier originators.
  • We favor bank originated collateral, defined vs. general purpose loans, short-dated consumer loans, and commercial obligor exposures. We’re constructive on select, higher-quality issuers across the capital stack within auto, consumer loans, and commercial sectors, offering favorable relative value within a global opportunity set.

Non-U.S. Bonds

Foreign Government Related (Current: 0.1%, Previous Quarter 0.1%)

  • As 2026 opens, we have a guardedly optimistic view on developed market rates, with a caveat. In Europe, long-term bunds have sold off, and we're monitoring whether yields may soon crest or if they are signaling a demand issue at the back of the curve. The ECB held its deposit rate at 2.0% to end 2025, and it expects an inflation undershoot in 2026 and 2027, which supports the potential for an additional 25 bp cut to 1.75% this year in what could represent the end of the easing cycle.
  • Policy easing should continue in the UK after the Bank of England's late-year rate cut brought its policy rate to 3.75%. Looking ahead, we see three additional 25 bp cuts in the first half of 2026 amidst the sizable slack in the UK economy, a budget that may limit inflation pressures, and potential improvement in the country's fiscal conditions in the first half of the year.
  • The steepening trend was clearly visible in the JGB market late last year as the Bank of Japan hiked its policy rate by 25 bps to 0.75%, marking the highest level in 30 years. The BoJ's statement that "real interest rates are at significantly low levels," indicated that it may be setting its sights on the lower end of its estimated neutral range of 1.0-2.5%, meaning that additional hikes may materialize in 2026. We anticipate further steepening along the 2- to 10-year portion of the JGB curve.

Emerging Markets Debt (EMD) (Current: 8.6%, Previous Quarter 10.6%)

  • EM Sovereigns/Spreads—The EM/DM growth differential should continue to play in EM's favor, even as China's growth slows. A growth differential of 2.5% or higher often leads to flows into EMD. Within the global backdrop, the extent to which slowing growth is still "good enough" and whether relative EM growth continues to attract flows, all matter. However, country-specific drivers should dominate in 2026. In a context where the Fed cuts rates and the U.S. fiscal outlook does not result in a massive bear steepening of the U.S. Treasury curve, EM hard currency returns could reach the high-single digit range with the appropriate identification of bottom-up alpha opportunities.
  • We continue to see attractive opportunities in BBs as they are well insulated and offer a yield cushion. "Stressed" BB names either have support from the U.S., other bilaterals, and/or multilaterals, as well as constructive fundamental dynamics.
  • Other geopolitical hotspots, including Ukraine and Venezuela, remain complicated. While vast uncertainty surrounds Venezuelan-related bonds, Maduro's ousting may be considered a market-positive event. For now, the current Venezuelan leadership may cooperate with the U.S. on potential investment in the oil sector and with immigration issues. At a minimum, this puts possible, yet complex, debt restructuring on the horizon
  • EMFX—We remain relative-value focused with the expectation that high-carry currencies will continue to outperform amid a broad-based weakening in the U.S. dollar due to a weakening U.S. labor market, additional Fed rate cuts, ample U.S. liquidity; a "Muddle Through" U.S. economy that should benefit high-carry currencies and EU fiscal measures that could offset growth downside and lead to higher growth vis-a-vis the U.S. Risks to this outlook include fewer Fed rate cuts than anticipated, a U.S. recession or global growth slowdown, increased geopolitical risks, and fiscal and election concerns in select countries, such as Brazil and Colombia.
  • EM Local Markets—After stellar performance in 2025, we have turned cautious on EM local rates. The most notable feature among many EM countries last year was monetary easing, bringing terminal rates closer to the lower end of the neutral rate zone. Brazil was the only EM country that hiked in 2025. Our base case in 2026 is that the majority of countries that cut rates last year are likely to stay on hold for the foreseeable future as reflected by the positive 2s5s spread on the swap curve.
  • EM Corporates—Spreads remained well anchored in Q4, driving positive returns. We believe this will likely remain the case in Q1 given the strong fundamentals and still elevated spread pick-up to developed market HY. While EM corporates significantly lagged sovereigns over the past quarter and year, this came after a period of strong outperformance. We expect overall defaults to remain contained in the 3% area for EM HY going forward (in line with developed markets) given very manageable refinancing needs and resiliency in the underlying EM economies.

U.S. Government Related

U.S. Treasuries (Current: 17.1%, Previous Quarter 9.5%)

  • The Fed's 25 bp rate cut in December carried a dovish tone, leading to a bull steepening along the curve with only slight movement in the 10-year yield. The narrow move on the 10-year underscored its prevailing low-volatility, range-bound conditions throughout 2025.
  • Thanks to the positive yield curve and a slight drop in yields, Treasuries outperformed cash and joined the bull market last year.
  • The Fed concluded its balance sheet normalization in December. In terms of supply, the U.S. Treasury has resisted calls to issue more long-term debt, instead opting to fund larger deficits through short-dated T-Bills, while also carrying out limited Treasury buybacks along the yield curve.

U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities (Current: 20.6%, Previous Quarter 26.1%)

  • Agency MBS posted positive excess returns vs. U.S. Treasuries in Q4, benefitting from lower volatility, improved demand, and light origination volumes despite the general decline in mortgage rates. Refinancing activity rose during the period and prepayment speeds increased—the brunt of which was borne by higher coupons within GNMA and conventional MBS.
  • The sector starts Q1 with tighter valuations relative to early Q4 due to easing Fed policy, lower volatility, constrained origination, and persistent demand for carry. While December's FOMC decision supported risk assets, further spread compressions will be difficult. We see a potential opportunity to outperform the benchmark index by avoiding the realized prepayment speeds in high coupon premiums.

data.title: Hacker Interstellar Files For IPO On Concerning User Trends
data.description:
Hacker Interstellar Is Producing Uneven Results

Hacker Interstellar Inc. (SOUD) has filed to raise $15 million in a U.S. IPO of its ordinary shares, according to an F-1 registration statement.

The company is developing social network voice and AI-related technologies for internet users in emerging markets.

However, in the most recent half-year period, user numbers are falling, as are ARPU and financial results.

I’ll provide an update when we gain more information about the IPO’s pricing and valuation.

What Does Hacker Interstellar Do?

The company is developing and commercializing a suite of voice-based AI technologies based on large language models (LLMs) and adapting them for vernacular languages and dialects, principally in Asian, Southeast Asian, and Middle-Eastern countries.

SOUD’s technologies include:

  • Voice social app - YoYo.

  • AI/LLM voice model technologies.

  • AI multilingual recommendation engine.

  • Virtual gifts & items.

  • Interactive virtual games.

A breakdown of the firm’s revenue contribution by type is shown in the pie chart below:

What Is Hacker Interstellar’s Market?

The AI voice generator market was an estimated $4.2 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $20.7 billion by 2031.

If achieved, this would represent a CAGR of 30.7% from 2025 to 2031, a very high rate of growth.

Expected drivers for this growth include organizations adopting voice technologies for low-latency speech, multilingual voice models, and real-time personalization.

Media platforms are projected to adopt these technologies to enhance engagement.

In 2025, North America was believed to hold the highest market share at around 41% of the total.

Hacker Interstellar is active on the consumer media subsegment and within emerging markets by region.

Competitors to the company include existing legacy social networks and local online social networking and entertainment industry market participants.

The firm’s strengths include its modified AI voice technology, localization focus (23 languages to date), strong user engagement of an average of 75 minutes per session, and internal AI automated operations system

Its weaknesses are its geographic concentration in politically and economically unstable regions, mobile platform dependency, and other concentration risks (supplier, technology)

Opportunities for the company include its plans to enter new regional markets, enterprise applications, and AI agent integrations

But SOUD faces ongoing uncertainties with the regulatory environment in China and intense competition from legacy players with large existing user bases.

Hacker Interstellar’s Recent Financial Results

The company's revenue growth has been uneven, falling YoY in the most recent half-year period, while gross profit and gross margin have also dropped recently.

Operating and net results have also trended lower, while cash flow from operations has bounced back in the most recent reporting period:

Hacker Interstellar’s IPO Plan

SOUD has filed to sell $15 million of its ordinary shares, although the final figure may change.

Since Nasdaq changed its minimum value of unrestricted publicly held shares (MVUPHS) from $5 million to $15 million on January 16, 2026, many small companies have been knocked out of the IPO process, and the remaining candidates have had to increase their IPOs to at least $15 million.

The firm, directors, and shareholders (5% or greater) will be subject to the standard 180-day shareholder sale lockup period after the effective date of the prospectus.

The company plans to use the IPO’s net proceeds as follows:

…approximately 50% of the net proceeds, for research and development of our AI agent products, including further development of our core AI algorithms, expansion of our R&D team, and product testing and iteration;

…approximately 30% of the net proceeds, for promotional and marketing activities to increase brand awareness and user acquisition;

…approximately 20% of the net proceeds, for research and development of other digital technologies and products.

(Source - SEC)

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Kingswood Capital Partners, LLC, and Longbridge.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of June 30, 2025, the company had $60 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments and $14 million in total liabilities.

For the trailing twelve months ended June 30, 2025, free cash flow was $15.7 million with no capital expenditures.

Its Selling and R&D spending have been modest for an AI social platform.

However, management will likely use some of the IPO proceeds for greater investments in R&D and potential capex.

The company's balance sheet is strong, with plenty of liquidity available for future growth initiatives, especially when the IPO’s net proceeds of around $11 million are added to its resources.

Hacker Interstellar Is Generating Uneven Results

The company is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its R&D and expansion initiatives.

Its growth plans include expanding regionally into markets such as Latin America and also into B2B applications for its AI-enabled voice technologies.

However, topline revenue has contracted in the most recent half-year period, as have gross profit and net results, while cash flow has been uneven as well.

Its broad market of AI-enabled voice applications is expected to grow at a very high rate of growth in the coming years, so the company enjoys positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

Hacker Interstellar is a ‘foreign private issuer’ and an ‘emerging growth company,’ which allows management to produce substantially less information for investors.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly in open market trading immediately after their IPO.

Additional risks include its exposure to Chinese government regulatory changes, which can be unpredictable and broadly market-changing.

It also has significant supplier concentration risks and payment platform risks.

Not only has SOUD generated uneven results, but its ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) has also dropped substantially in the most recent reporting period:
